title: Build a REST API with Node.js
subtitle: Hands-on guide to designing and shipping a production-ready API
description: Go from zero to a deployed REST API using Node.js, Express, and PostgreSQL through a series of practical exercises.
targetAudience: Junior developers comfortable with JavaScript who want backend experience
objectives: Design a resource-oriented REST API following conventions
Implement CRUD endpoints with Express and a PostgreSQL database
Write automated tests for every route
Deploy the API to a cloud platform

modules.title: Project Setup
modules.description: Scaffold the project, configure tooling, and connect to the database.

lessons.title: Initialise Express
lessons.description: Create a new Node.js project and install Express.
lessons.content: Run npm init, install express and nodemon, then write a minimal server that responds with 200 OK on GET /health.
keyPoints: npm init -y creates package.json quickly
nodemon restarts the server on file changes
app.listen binds to a port and accepts connections

lessons.title: Environment Variables
lessons.description: Keep secrets out of source code with dotenv.
lessons.content: Store database credentials and API keys in a .env file; dotenv loads them into process.env at startup — never commit .env to git.
keyPoints: dotenv.config() must run before any process.env access
.env.example documents required vars without values
CI platforms inject vars via environment, not a file

lessons.title: Connect to PostgreSQL
lessons.description: Use the pg package to open a connection pool.
lessons.content: Instantiate a pg Pool with DATABASE_URL from process.env and export a query helper used by all route handlers.
keyPoints: Pool reuses connections instead of opening a new one per query
Always release the client back to the pool
Use parameterised queries to prevent SQL injection

lessons.title: Database Migrations
lessons.description: Version-control your schema with db-migrate.
lessons.content: Write an UP migration that creates a users table, and a DOWN migration that drops it — run migrations before each test suite.
keyPoints: Migrations are repeatable and reversible
Never alter a committed migration — write a new one
Run migrations in CI before integration tests

quiz.question: Why should you never commit a .env file to git?
options: dotenv does not work from a git repository
It contains secrets that would be exposed publicly
quiz.explanation: A .env file holds database passwords and API keys — pushing it to a public repo exposes those credentials immediately.

modules.title: CRUD Endpoints
modules.description: Implement Create, Read, Update, and Delete routes for a resource.
objectives: Map HTTP methods to CRUD operations

lessons.title: GET — List and Retrieve
lessons.description: Implement GET /items and GET /items/:id.
lessons.content: GET /items returns a JSON array; GET /items/:id returns a single object or 404 if not found — always set Content-Type: application/json.
keyPoints: Use 200 for success, 404 when the record is missing
Parse req.params.id and validate it is a positive integer
SELECT ... LIMIT for list endpoints prevents returning millions of rows

lessons.title: POST — Create
lessons.description: Accept a request body, validate it, and insert a new row.
lessons.content: Parse the JSON body with express.json(), validate required fields with joi, insert into the database, and return 201 Created with the new record.
keyPoints: express.json() middleware must be registered before route handlers
Return 400 Bad Request when validation fails
INSERT ... RETURNING * gives back the inserted row without a second query

lessons.title: PUT — Replace
lessons.description: Replace an existing resource with the full updated body.
lessons.content: PUT /items/:id overwrites all mutable fields; if the record does not exist return 404, not 200.
keyPoints: PUT is idempotent — calling it twice has the same effect as once
Validate all fields, not just the changed ones
Use RETURNING * instead of a follow-up SELECT

lessons.title: DELETE — Remove
lessons.description: Delete a record and handle the not-found case.
lessons.content: DELETE /items/:id removes the row; return 204 No Content on success and 404 if the record is absent.
keyPoints: 204 responses must not include a body
Check rowCount from pg to detect missing records
Soft deletes (setting deleted_at) are often safer than hard deletes in production

lessons.title: Input Validation with Joi
lessons.description: Define schemas to reject invalid payloads early.
lessons.content: A Joi schema describes the expected shape of a request body; abortEarly: false collects all errors at once so the client can fix them in one round trip.
keyPoints: Joi.object().required() makes the entire body mandatory
Use .messages() to write user-friendly error strings
Centralise schemas in a validators/ directory

quiz.question: Which HTTP status code should a DELETE endpoint return on success?
quiz.explanation: 204 No Content signals success without a response body, which is appropriate for DELETE.

modules.title: Authentication
modules.description: Secure your API with JWT-based authentication.
objectives: Hash passwords with bcrypt before storing them
Protect routes with an auth middleware

lessons.title: Password Hashing
lessons.description: Store passwords safely with bcrypt.
lessons.content: Never store plain-text passwords — bcrypt hashes them with a salt so that even a database breach does not expose user credentials.
keyPoints: bcrypt.hash() takes the plain password and a cost factor
Cost factor 12 is a reasonable default for 2026 hardware
bcrypt.compare() verifies a plain password against a stored hash

lessons.title: Issuing JWTs
lessons.description: Return a signed token on successful login.
lessons.content: After verifying credentials, sign a JWT containing the user's id and role, then return it so the client can attach it to future requests.
keyPoints: jwt.sign() needs a secret stored in an env var, never hardcoded
Set a short expiry (e.g. 1h) and issue refresh tokens separately
Store the JWT on the client in memory, not localStorage, to reduce XSS risk

lessons.title: Auth Middleware
lessons.description: Verify the JWT on protected routes.
lessons.content: Write a middleware that reads the Authorization header, verifies the JWT, and attaches the decoded payload to req.user — return 401 on any failure.
keyPoints: Always return 401, never 403, when the token is missing or invalid
Use a try/catch around jwt.verify() to handle malformed tokens
Attach only safe, non-secret fields to req.user

lessons.title: Role-Based Access
lessons.description: Restrict certain endpoints to users with the right role.
lessons.content: A requireRole middleware reads req.user.role set by the auth middleware and returns 403 Forbidden if the caller lacks permission.
keyPoints: Run auth middleware before requireRole
Keep roles simple: user and admin cover most APIs
Return 403 (Forbidden) not 401 when the user is authenticated but lacks permission

quiz.question: What HTTP status code should an auth middleware return when a JWT is missing?
quiz.explanation: 401 Unauthorized means the request lacks valid authentication credentials — the client should log in and retry.

modules.title: Testing and Deployment
modules.description: Write automated tests and deploy to a cloud platform.
objectives: Run tests in CI with GitHub Actions
Deploy the API with a Dockerfile

lessons.title: Integration Tests with Supertest
lessons.description: Test each route end-to-end against a real database.
lessons.content: Supertest spins up the Express app in-process and sends real HTTP requests — no mocking needed for straightforward CRUD tests.
keyPoints: Use a dedicated test database separate from development
Truncate tables in beforeEach to isolate test cases
Test both the happy path and every error branch

lessons.title: GitHub Actions CI
lessons.description: Run tests automatically on every pull request.
lessons.content: A GitHub Actions workflow spins up a Postgres service container, runs migrations, then executes npm test — PRs cannot merge while tests are red.
keyPoints: Use services: postgres: in the workflow YAML to get a throwaway database
Cache node_modules with actions/cache to speed up runs
Set required status checks in branch protection rules

lessons.title: Dockerfile
lessons.description: Package the API as a container image.
lessons.content: A multi-stage Dockerfile installs dependencies in a build stage, then copies only the production artefacts to a lean runtime image — reducing the final image size.
keyPoints: Use node:20-alpine as the runtime base to keep images small
COPY package*.json first to maximise layer caching
Never run the process as root inside the container

lessons.title: Deploy to Railway
lessons.description: Ship the containerised API to a managed cloud platform.
lessons.content: Railway detects your Dockerfile, provisions a managed Postgres database, and wires DATABASE_URL automatically — one git push deploys the API.
keyPoints: Pin a specific image tag in production, never use :latest
Use Railway's env var UI to inject secrets
Enable health-check probes so Railway can restart crashed instances automatically

quiz.question: Why should tests use a separate database instead of the development database?
options: Tests run faster on a dedicated database
The development database does not support SQL
Tests truncate tables and would destroy development data
Supertest cannot connect to a development database
quiz.explanation: Test suites routinely wipe and re-seed tables — doing that against the development database would destroy your local data.

modules.title: Going Further
modules.description: Production patterns: pagination, rate limiting, and observability.

lessons.title: Pagination
lessons.description: Return large lists in manageable pages.
lessons.content: Cursor-based pagination uses an opaque cursor (usually the last row's id) instead of page numbers — it stays consistent even when rows are inserted between requests.
keyPoints: Offset pagination breaks when rows are inserted mid-page
Return a nextCursor in the response; null means no more pages
Use LIMIT n+1 to detect whether another page exists without a COUNT query

lessons.title: Rate Limiting
lessons.description: Protect your API from abuse with request throttling.
lessons.content: express-rate-limit tracks requests per IP and returns 429 Too Many Requests when the threshold is exceeded — apply stricter limits to auth endpoints.
keyPoints: Set windowMs and max to define the throttle window
Use a Redis store for rate limits in multi-instance deployments
429 responses should include Retry-After in the header

lessons.title: Structured Logging with Pino
lessons.description: Emit JSON logs that are easy to search and alert on.
lessons.content: Pino writes newline-delimited JSON instead of plain strings — log aggregators like Datadog or CloudWatch can parse fields and build dashboards from them.
keyPoints: Always include a request id in every log line
Log at the right level: debug in dev, info/warn/error in production
Never log sensitive data (passwords, tokens, PII)

quiz.question: What HTTP status code does a rate limiter return when the threshold is exceeded?
quiz.explanation: 429 Too Many Requests signals that the client has sent too many requests in a given timeframe.

bonusResources.title: Node.js Best Practices
bonusResources.description: Community-maintained list of Node.js production patterns.
